ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper advances a notable and clearly differentiated perspective: a no-training UD parser that leverages UD constraints, personalized PageRank for content-word ranking, a two-step decoder enforcing function-word leafness, and on-the-fly adposition direction estimation — ideas not combined in prior work on delexicalized transfer. These components constitute a non-trivial methodological departure from multi-source transfer and adjacency baselines rather than a mere rehash, and the test-time ADP direction estimation is particularly novel. That said, many subcomponents (head-rule constraints, PageRank, decoding heuristics) are adaptations of existing techniques, so the contribution is innovative but not radically groundbreaking. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful synthesis: a truly training-free UD parser that leverages UD POS constraints, a small set of head rules, personalized PageRank, and a two-step decoding that enforces function words as leaves—an approach that is clearly distinguished from supervised and delexicalized transfer baselines. While the components (head-rule heuristics, graph-ranking like PageRank, and function-word constraints) are known individually, their combination into a language-agnostic, out-of-the-box UD parser and the pragmatic test-time estimation of adposition directionality is a non-trivial and novel engineering contribution that would be informative to experts focused on deployment across many languages and domains.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a notable and clearly differentiated perspective: a no-training UD parser that leverages UD constraints, personalized PageRank for content-word ranking, a two-step decoder enforcing function-word leafness, and on-the-fly adposition direction estimation — ideas not combined in prior work on delexicalized transfer. These components constitute a non-trivial methodological departure from multi-source transfer and adjacency baselines rather than a mere rehash, and the test-time ADP direction estimation is particularly novel. That said, many subcomponents (head-rule constraints, PageRank, decoding heuristics) are adaptations of existing techniques, so the contribution is innovative but not radically groundbreaking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful synthesis: a truly training-free UD parser that leverages UD POS constraints, a small set of head rules, personalized PageRank, and a two-step decoding that enforces function words as leaves—an approach that is clearly distinguished from supervised and delexicalized transfer baselines. While the components (head-rule heuristics, graph-ranking like PageRank, and function-word constraints) are known individually, their combination into a language-agnostic, out-of-the-box UD parser and the pragmatic test-time estimation of adposition directionality is a non-trivial and novel engineering contribution that would be informative to experts focused on deployment across many languages and domains.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper provides hyperparameters and model configurations and reports UAS evaluation; datasets are standard UD 1.2 treebanks (plus clearly cited supplementary sets) and the authors release code, which strongly supports reproducibility. However, the summary suggests several implementation-level details are underspecified in the text (exact directed multigraph construction, PPR personalization/damping/iteration settings, precise two-step decoding algorithm and ADP direction estimation protocol), and the preprocessing pipeline (token/MWE handling, POS tag normalization) is not fully spelled out. Given the available code this is mostly reproducible, but from the paper alone a reimplementation would require clarifying these operational specifics. | B(4): Overall the paper is mostly reproducible: code is available, hyperparameters are reported, datasets are standard UD treebanks (UD 1.2 etc.) and evaluation uses UAS which is clearly defined. The method description (head-rule multigraph, personalized PageRank, two-step decoding, estimating adposition direction on test data) is concrete enough to reimplement, but the summary suggests some implementation-level details (exact graph edge weighting, PageRank damping and initialization specifics, any preprocessing/token normalization, and handling of MWE/coordination) are not fully enumerated. Given those minor but potentially important omissions, I judge it mostly reproducible with a few details missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides hyperparameters and model configurations and reports UAS evaluation; datasets are standard UD 1.2 treebanks (plus clearly cited supplementary sets) and the authors release code, which strongly supports reproducibility. However, the summary suggests several implementation-level details are underspecified in the text (exact directed multigraph construction, PPR personalization/damping/iteration settings, precise two-step decoding algorithm and ADP direction estimation protocol), and the preprocessing pipeline (token/MWE handling, POS tag normalization) is not fully spelled out. Given the available code this is mostly reproducible, but from the paper alone a reimplementation would require clarifying these operational specifics.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is mostly reproducible: code is available, hyperparameters are reported, datasets are standard UD treebanks (UD 1.2 etc.) and evaluation uses UAS which is clearly defined. The method description (head-rule multigraph, personalized PageRank, two-step decoding, estimating adposition direction on test data) is concrete enough to reimplement, but the summary suggests some implementation-level details (exact graph edge weighting, PageRank damping and initialization specifics, any preprocessing/token normalization, and handling of MWE/coordination) are not fully enumerated. Given those minor but potentially important omissions, I judge it mostly reproducible with a few details missing.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (zero-training UDP, PPR ranking, two-step decoding, ADP direction estimation), with experiments and hyperparameters reported and code released. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures/tables and examples seem informative and writing quality is described as good. From a formalist perspective, a couple of methodological descriptions (notably the ADP direction estimation and the two-step decoding) could benefit from tighter, formal exposition and clearer links to failure modes or guarantees, and stronger ablation references to aid reproducibility. These are minor presentation issues rather than pervasive clarity problems, so I rate it a 4. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—presenting a training-free UDP using UD head rules, personalized PageRank, and a two-step decoder. Notation and methodology are described concretely (directed multigraph, PageRank personalization, adposition direction estimation), figures/tables/examples seem effective and datasets/baselines are comprehensive (32 UD languages, delexicalized transfer, adjacency baselines). Writing quality is reported as good with code and hyperparameters available, though some sections (e.g., limitations on MWE/coordination and extreme coarse POS degradation) suggest places where additional clarity or expanded discussion would help.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (zero-training UDP, PPR ranking, two-step decoding, ADP direction estimation), with experiments and hyperparameters reported and code released. Notation appears consistent and related work coverage is thorough; figures/tables and examples seem informative and writing quality is described as good. From a formalist perspective, a couple of methodological descriptions (notably the ADP direction estimation and the two-step decoding) could benefit from tighter, formal exposition and clearer links to failure modes or guarantees, and stronger ablation references to aid reproducibility. These are minor presentation issues rather than pervasive clarity problems, so I rate it a 4.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early—presenting a training-free UDP using UD head rules, personalized PageRank, and a two-step decoder. Notation and methodology are described concretely (directed multigraph, PageRank personalization, adposition direction estimation), figures/tables/examples seem effective and datasets/baselines are comprehensive (32 UD languages, delexicalized transfer, adjacency baselines). Writing quality is reported as good with code and hyperparameters available, though some sections (e.g., limitations on MWE/coordination and extreme coarse POS degradation) suggest places where additional clarity or expanded discussion would help.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is highly relevant to the venue—addressing multilingual, low-resource dependency parsing with broad evaluation, released code, and practical contributions. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about missing rigorous ablations, sensitivity analyses, and statistical significance testing are substantial methodological gaps for a top conference; these limitations prevent a full top score despite the strong practical impact highlighted by Reviewer B.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—unsupervised dependency parsing for UD—is squarely within a top-tier NLP venue and addresses the right audience interested in cross-lingual and domain-robust parsing. However, from the summary I must penalise methodological gaps that top conferences expect: the paper presents heuristic components (PPR ranking, two-step decoding, adposition direction estimation) but appears to lack formal guarantees or thorough ablation studies quantifying each component’s contribution, and it does not report statistical significance testing for cross-lingual comparisons. The code release, clear hyperparameter reporting, broad UD evaluation, and robustness claims make it a strong candidate, but the absence of rigorous ablations and sensitivity analyses (e.g., to noisy POS) prevents a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (dependency parsing) with a novel, practically-oriented approach that requires no treebank training and demonstrates broad multilingual and cross-domain evaluation across 32 UD languages—well within the scope and interests of top NLP venues. The methodology (head-rule constraints, personalized PageRank, test-time adposition direction estimation) and comparisons to strong delexicalized baselines and adjacency heuristics meet community standards for evaluation and reproducibility (code available). From my pragmatic multilingualist perspective, the emphasis on out-of-the-box, low-resource applicability and robustness to domain/POS degradation makes it especially relevant to the venue’s audience. Limitations (UAS-only, coarse-POS sensitivity, unresolved MWE/coordination issues) are acknowledged and do not undermine the paper’s strong fit for the conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides strong multilingual and domain-robust empirical evidence, releases code and hyperparameters, and candidly discusses limitations. Deductions reflect missing finer-grained analyses (more ablations, statistical testing, labeled-dependency evaluation and per-language error breakdowns), but these are refinements rather than fatal flaws, so the paper merits a solid score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a broad multilingual evaluation (32 UD languages), domain-robustness tests and reports hyperparameters and code, which is adequate evidence for its main claims. However, from the summary I see no systematic ablation quantifying the individual contributions of core algorithmic components (PPR ranking, two-step decoding, ADP direction estimation), no statistical significance testing across languages, and only a limited sensitivity study to POS noise (extreme coarse tags only). The related work appears thorough and limitations are acknowledged, but the methodological guarantees and finer-grained failure analyses that my reviewer persona requires are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents broad multilingual and domain-robust experiments (32 UD languages, domain-shift tests, POS degradation scenarios) and compares against reasonable baselines including multi-source delexicalized transfer and adjacency heuristics, which supports the main claims well. It includes sensitivity analyses (POS degradation, adposition direction estimation) and reports hyperparameters and released code, aiding reproducibility. Related work appears thorough and the authors candidly discuss limitations (no labeled attachments, handling of MWEs/coordination, severe drop with coarse POS). I dock one point because the evaluation omits labeled dependencies and some failure modes (e.g., detailed per-language error analyses or more ablations of the decoding choices) could be expanded to fully convince across diverse syntactic phenomena.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear empirical claims supported by broad multilingual experiments and reasonable baselines, and that core algorithmic pieces are conceptually sound and reproducible. However, they also note important methodological gaps—no statistical significance testing, limited ablation studies, and incomplete analysis of POS/noise sensitivity and certain constructions—which reduce confidence in some robustness claims. Given the solid empirical contribution but these notable evaluation omissions, a score slightly above the midpoint is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear empirical claims and supports them with cross-lingual experiments against reasonable baselines (multi-source delexicalized transfer, adjacency baselines) across 32 UD languages, but the summary indicates important methodological omissions: no statistical significance testing is reported and there is a lack of systematic ablation to quantify contributions of PPR ranking, two-step decoding, and ADP direction estimation. There are also unstated formal properties for the core algorithmic steps (no correctness/consistency guarantees for the two-step decoding or PPR-based ranking) and potential sensitivity to POS quality is only partially explored (extreme coarse POS tested but no graded noise analysis). Given these gaps, the results are promising but some claims (robustness to domain/POS degradation and component-level contributions) are insufficiently supported by the evidence provided.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by multilingual experiments (32 UD languages), comparisons to reasonable baselines (multi-source delexicalized transfer, adjacency baselines, rule baseline), and analyses of domain and POS degradation; code and hyperparameters are provided which aids reproducibility. Mathematical components (PageRank, head-rule constraints) are straightforward and appear correct; however the evaluation is limited to UAS (no labeled dependencies) and the extreme coarse-POS failure is acknowledged but not fully mitigated. I would have liked explicit statistical testing and more analysis on constructions the method fails on (coordination, MWEs), but these are minor relative to the core claims that an unsupervised, no-training parser can be competitive and robust across languages and domains.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper evaluates on appropriate UD test sets with sensible, strong baselines and reports per-language UAS results, making the empirical comparison broadly fair and thorough. However, Reviewer A’s point about the lack of statistical significance testing and systematic ablations of key components is valid and warrants a minor deduction, so I give a 4 to reflect solid comparative evaluation but missing some robustness analyses.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible baselines (multi-source delexicalized transfer, a rule-based head-rule baseline, and adjacency baselines) on standard UD test sets using UAS and reports per-language results, which is generally fair. It also acknowledges when prior methods are competitive and states limitations. However, from the provided summary I see no mention of statistical significance testing or systematic cross-language ablation of the key components (PPR ranking, two-step decoding, ADP direction estimation), which weakens claims of superiority across languages and conditions—hence a minor deduction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares UDP against appropriate and strong baselines including multi-source delexicalized transfer (TurboParser), a rule-based head-rule baseline, and adjacency baselines across 32 UD languages on UD 1.2 test sets using UAS, which is the right metric and dataset for this setting. Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), the authors acknowledge when prior methods outperform theirs and discuss limitations (e.g., coarse POS drop, unlabeled evaluation), and they analyze differences (head-rule graph + PageRank vs. delex transfer) while citing related work thoroughly. From my pragmatic multilingual perspective, this is a comprehensive and well-justified empirical comparison for an out-of-the-box, no-training parser.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and provides a simple, interpretable, and robust no-training approach that is competitive across many languages and domains. Its likely practical utility for lightweight, deployable parsers justifies above-average impact, though the scope is tempered by UAS-only evaluation, sensitivity to very coarse POS, and missing analyses of label/MWE handling. Overall, the work is expected to stimulate follow-up research without being transformational, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—building dependency parsers without training—and demonstrates competitive UAS against multi-source delexicalized transfer across 32 UD languages, showing robustness to domain shift and degrading POS. From my perspective, this is a useful and practical contribution that will provoke follow-up work (e.g., integrating labeling, handling MWEs/coordination, and formal analyses of guarantees), but it is not transformative: core limitations (UAS-only evaluation, sensitivity to extremely coarse POS, and missing analyses/ablations of algorithmic components) constrain its broader impact. I expect it to stimulate research on lightweight, language-agnostic parsing methods but not to fundamentally change parsing paradigms on its own.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—dependency parsing without any treebank training—and presents a simple, interpretable method (UD head rules + personalized PageRank + two-step decoding) that is surprisingly competitive with multi-source delexicalized transfer across 32 UD languages and more robust to domain and POS degradation. From my perspective as a pragmatic multilingualist, the no-training design and strong cross-lingual/domain robustness make this likely to change how people think about lightweight, deployable parsers for low-resource settings and rapid domain adaptation. The work opens useful directions (unsupervised estimation of attachment directions, test-time heuristics) and is likely to be built upon, though its impact is tempered by focusing only on UAS, ignoring labels and MWEs, and by degraded performance with very coarse POS tags.

main_claims: A dependency parser (UDP) that requires no training and relies solely on UD POS constraints and a small set of head rules is presented.
UDP uses personalized PageRank to rank content words and a two-step decoding enforcing function words as leaves, estimating adposition attachment direction directly on test data.
UDP is competitive with multi-source delexicalized transfer (delexicalized parsing) across 32 UD languages and is more robust to domain changes and deteriorating POS tags.
The parser outperforms adjacency baselines and, in at least one case (Hindi), outperforms a multi-source delexicalized supervised system.
_summary.methodology: Build a directed multigraph per sentence using UD-compliant head rules, personalize PageRank (with higher weight on an estimated main predicate) to rank content words, decode attachments in two steps ensuring function words are leaves, and estimate adposition directionality from test data (ADP-NOMINAL vs NOMINAL-ADP counts).
datasets: Annotated sets: first 50 verses of the King James Bible, 50 magazine sentences, 75 sentences from QuestionBank test split
baselines: Multi-source delexicalized transfer parser (delexicalized TurboParser / multi-dir / MSD)
limitations: Large drop in performance under the extreme coarse POS scenario (content vs. function tags).
Only unlabeled attachment was considered (no labeled dependencies).
Does not handle multiword expressions, coordination, and proper names (stated as future work).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - unsupervised dependency parsing / Universal Dependencies

reviewer_a.name: Formalist Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in computational linguistics with a PhD in formal syntax and 12+ years reviewing parsing/grammar induction work.
traits: Penalises papers that present heuristic algorithms without formal properties: expects at least discussion of correctness, consistency, or failure modes for core algorithmic steps.
Penalises empirical papers that lack rigorous significance testing: deducts points when no statistical tests or confidence intervals are reported for cross-lingual comparisons.

Requires strong ablations: penalises when a paper does not quantify contribution of each component (e.g., PPR ranking, two-step decoding, ADP direction estimation) via systematic ablation on at least a subset of languages.
Penalises papers that rely heavily on gold or high-quality POS tags without studying sensitivity: expects explicit analysis of how performance degrades with noisier POS inputs.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on theoretical grounding and methodological rigor: I reward clear guarantees, careful ablation, and statistical validation, and I penalise heuristic-only claims that lack such analyses.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Multilingualist
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP scientist with experience deploying multilingual and low-resource systems; co-author of multiple papers on cross-lingual transfer and UD parsing.
traits: Rewards methods requiring no supervised training or treebanks: gives positive credit to approaches that work 'out of the box' across many languages and domains.
Values broad multilingual and domain-robust evaluations: rewards papers that evaluate on many UD treebanks and demonstrate robustness to domain shifts and degraded inputs.
Rewards simple, interpretable algorithms with few tunable hyperparameters: favors solutions that are easy to deploy and analyze (e.g., rule-based head constraints + PageRank).
Requires a reasonable set of baselines but is forgiving about heavy modeling complexity: will accept heuristic components if empirical gains across languages are convincing.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I favour practical, robust, and widely-applicable solutions: I prioritise multilingual coverage, domain robustness, and methods that enable parsing without annotated resources.
